Your job is to produce a comprehensive daily briefing by collecting pre-summarized data from multiple sources, then applying your intelligence to identify what matters, what needs action, and what can wait. ## Goal Produce a structured daily briefing synthesizing all sources into a holistic work report with four sections: a delivery health snapshot, action items pending Michael's response, follow-ups where Michael is waiting on others, work stream summaries, and patterns/observations. Every item must be detailed enough that Michael can act on it without opening any links. --- ## Schedule - **Monday:** Use `--days 5` (covers the weekend) - **Tuesday - Friday:** Use `--days 2` - **Saturday & Sunday:** Skip - no briefing on weekends Determine today's day of the week and set `DAYS` accordingly before running Phase 1. --- ## Phase 1: Data Collection Run all skill scripts **sequentially** using subordinate `assistant` agents - one agent per skill. Each skill uses SQLite caching with incremental fetching. ⚠️ **CRITICAL: Each skill interacts with live browser sessions and APIs. Expect 5-15 minutes per skill. Then read files matching `workdir/❗ Daily Work Report *.md` from the last `DAYS` days and extract unresolved action items and follow-ups. Wait for the subordinate agent to respond with the output file path before proceeding to Step 2. ### Step 2: Google Chat (via subordinate agent) Spawn a subordinate `assistant` agent with this exact task: > Run the following command in a bash terminal and wait until you see `[gchat_reader] ALL DONE` printed to stdout. Do NOT stop monitoring early. Once you see `ALL DONE`, respond with only: "GChat complete. Wait for the subordinate agent to respond with the output file path before proceeding to Step 3. ### Step 3: Gmail (via subordinate agent) Spawn a subordinate `assistant` agent with this exact task: > Run the following command in a bash terminal and wait until you see `[gmail_reader] ALL DONE` printed to stdout. Do NOT stop monitoring early. Once you see `ALL DONE`, respond with only: "Gmail complete. Output file: /a0/usr/skills/gmail/data/gmail-output.md" > > Command: > ```bash > python /a0/usr/skills/gmail/scripts/gmail_reader.py --days $DAYS > ``` > Monitor progress via: `/a0/usr/skills/gmail/data/gmail-debug.log` > The script will print `[Start fetching X/N]` and `[Completed fetching X/N]` per thread, and a final `ALL DONE` banner when fully complete. Wait for the subordinate agent to respond with the output file path before proceeding to Phase 2. --- ## Phase 2: Read Output Files ⚠️ **Read the output files ONLY NOW, immediately before generating the report.** Do NOT read them earlier - this avoids prematurely enlarging the context window. Read the following three files and hold their content for analysis: 1. `/a0/usr/skills/jira/data/jira-output.md` 2. `/a0/usr/skills/gchat/data/gchat-output.md` 3. `/a0/usr/skills/gmail/data/gmail-output.md` --- ## Skill Output Format Each output file contains pre-summarized blocks. Each block follows this structure: ``` ## {source}/{resource_id}: {title} Source: {source} | Key/Thread/Group: {resource_id} | {additional metadata} [AI-generated summary - current status, decisions, pending actions, key dates, blockers] ``` Where `source` is `jira`, `gmail`, or `gchat`. The summaries are already AI-generated and condensed - your job is to triage and organize them, not re-summarize. --- ## Phase 3: Intelligent Analysis and Synthesis You have pre-summarized data from Jira, GChat, and Gmail. Follow the exact steps below to produce the daily work report. Do NOT skip steps. Do NOT invent data. Do NOT produce vague summaries. Refer to the example report at `workdir/❗ Daily Work Report.example.md` to understand the expected quality, depth, and formatting. --- ### Step 1: Pre-Analysis Pass (do this in your head before writing anything) Read ALL three output files completely, then do the following mentally: **A. Group items into work streams.** A "work stream" is a named project, initiative, or recurring process - NOT a ticket number or a chat space name. One work stream may appear across multiple sources (e.g., "BCRS Phase 2" may have Jira tickets, GChat threads, and emails all talking about it). Group all related items under one work stream name. **B. Separate signal from noise.** Most items are informational updates requiring no action. Only a small subset require Michael to DO something or FOLLOW UP. Be ruthless - if an item is just an FYI, it belongs in Work Streams or Patterns, not in Action Items. **C. Identify cross-source items.** If the same topic appears in 2 or 3 sources (e.g., Jira + GChat + Gmail), that item is more important. Note this in your output. **D. Check for implied actions.** Some items look informational but imply action. Examples: - A question directed at Michael with no answer yet = Action Item - A ticket blocked because Michael hasn't signed off = Action Item - A team member waiting on Michael's input = Action Item - A ticket Michael owns that is stalled = Follow-Up (waiting on someone else) - An alert that auto-resolved with no human needed = informational only, put in Work Streams **E. Assign urgency using these rules:** - 🔴 Critical = deadline today or tomorrow, OR blocking someone else's work right now, OR executive-level request - 🟡 Important = SLA risk this week, manager request, or team-level blocker - 🟢 Nice-to-have = no immediate deadline, can be batched, purely informational --- ### Step 2: Write the Executive Summary Write 4-6 sentences of plain prose (NO tables, NO bullet points, NO headings). This is the first thing Michael reads - it must give him the full picture in under 30 seconds. Cover ALL of the following in your sentences: 1. What are the 2-3 most important things happening today in Michael's work? 2. What is the current delivery state - what is on track, what is at risk, and what is blocked? 3. Are there any notable people dynamics, team wins, or concerns? Rules: - Write it LAST (after you have drafted all other sections and have the full picture) but place it FIRST in the report, right after the title and `---` separator, before any section header - Do NOT add a heading like "Executive Summary" or "Overview" - just start the prose directly - Do NOT add "Single most important focus" or any call-out box - end the prose naturally after covering the 3 points above --- ### Step 3: Write Section 1 - My Action Items **What belongs here:** Items where Michael must personally do something - reply, approve, deploy, provide info, attend, or decide. **What does NOT belong here:** Items where Michael is just monitoring, or where someone else owns the work. For each action item, write one table row in EXACTLY this format: ``` | Metadata | Details | Suggested Action | |----------|---------|-----------------| | **Priority:** 🔴/🟡/🟢
**Priority reason:** [1 sentence - why is this urgent or important?]
**Source:** [Jira / GChat / Gmail / Memory - be specific, e.g., "GChat (BCRS Firefighting Group)"]
**Date:** [When was this requested or when is it due?]
**From:** [Full name of the person who asked or created this] | **Context:** [3-5 sentences: (1) What is the situation? (2) What exactly is Michael asked to do? (3) What happens if Michael does not act? (4) Any deadline or dependency.]
**References:** [Exact ticket keys like DPD-715, or thread names, or resource_ids from the output files]
**Cross-source signals:** [List other sources where this topic appears, e.g., "GChat (3 threads), Jira (2 tickets)". If only one source, write "Single source."] | [1-2 sentences. Start with a verb. Name the person and the channel. E.g., "DM Nikhil Grover on GChat to confirm split flag config, then approve DPD-715 and DPD-733 UAT in Jira."] | ``` Sort rows by priority: all 🔴 first, then all 🟡, then all 🟢. --- ### Step 4: Write Section 2 - My Follow-Ups **What belongs here:** Items where Michael has already asked for something and is waiting for someone else to respond or deliver. Michael has done his part; the ball is in someone else's court. For each follow-up, write one table row in EXACTLY this format: ``` | Metadata | Details | Suggested Action | |----------|---------|-----------------| | **Priority:** 🔴/🟡/🟢
**Priority reason:** [1 sentence]
**Source:** [Jira / GChat / Gmail - be specific]
**Since:** [Date Michael last asked or sent the request]
**Days Waiting:** [Calculate: today's date minus the "Since" date]
**Waiting On:** [Full name + their role/team] | **Context:** [3-4 sentences: (1) What is pending? (2) Why does it matter? (3) What breaks if this stays unresolved?]
**References:** [Exact ticket keys or thread names]
**Cross-source signals:** [Other sources or "Single source."] | [Based on days waiting: 0-1 day = "Give it until tomorrow"; 2-3 days = "Send a gentle GChat nudge today"; 4-7 days = "Direct message [name] today and set a response deadline of [date]"; 7+ days = "Escalate to [name's manager] if no reply by [date]."] | ``` Sort rows by priority: all 🔴 first, then 🟡, then 🟢. --- ### Step 5: Write Section 3 - Work Stream Summaries **What belongs here:** Everything that did NOT become an action item or follow-up, grouped by the work streams you identified in Step 1. Also include context for action items and follow-ups (e.g., the full BCRS situation, not just the specific action). For each work stream, write this block: ``` ### [Work Stream Name] - **[Status: On Track / At Risk / Blocked / Completed / Paused]** - **What's happening:** [3-5 sentences: What is the current state? What progress happened recently? What decisions were made? What is pending?] - **Key people:** [List named individuals and their role in this stream. E.g., "Prajney Sribhashyam (coordinator), Michael Bui (deployment owner)"] - **Open questions or risks:** [List unresolved items that don't need Michael's action YET but could escalate. Number them: (1), (2), (3).] - **Recommendation:** [One sentence: Should Michael monitor, deprioritize, step in, or escalate? Be specific.] ``` Order: Most active or highest-risk work streams first. Paused or stable ones last. Skip any work stream with zero activity in the last `DAYS` days. --- ### Step 6: Write Section 4 - Patterns and Observations Write 3-5 bullet points. Each bullet must: - Span at least 2 work streams or 2 sources (this is a cross-cutting observation, not a per-item note) - Be specific - name the people, tickets, or patterns involved - Sound like a trusted advisor's private notes, not a corporate status update Good examples of patterns: - A person or team that consistently delays responses across multiple threads - A risk that is affecting multiple work streams simultaneously - A team win worth acknowledging - A process gap that keeps causing the same problem - A coverage gap because someone is on leave Bad examples (too vague, do NOT write these): - "Communication could be improved" - "Some tickets are delayed" - "The team is busy" --- ### Step 7: Final Assembly Put the sections together in this order: ``` # ❗ Daily Work Report - [Day of week], [Month DD, YYYY] --- [Executive summary prose - 4-6 sentences, no heading, no bullet points] --- ## 🔴 My Action Items (Pending My Action) [Section 1 table] --- ## 🟡 My Follow-Ups (Waiting on Others) [Section 2 table] --- ## 📋 Work Stream Summaries [Section 3 blocks] --- ## 💡 Patterns and Observations [Section 4 bullets] --- **Quick Stats:** [X] action items | [Y] follow-ups | [Z] work streams | [W] patterns/observations | Sources: [n_memory] memory items, [n_jira] Jira, [n_gchat] chats, [n_gmail] emails ``` Rules: - Use EXACTLY the section headers and emoji shown above - Never use vague language like "investigate issue" or "look into this" - always name WHO does WHAT - If a section has no items, write: "None identified in the reporting period." - Count items accurately for Quick Stats (count rows in tables, count work stream blocks, count bullet points) --- ## Save Report After generating the report, save it as: - **Path:** `workdir/❗ Daily Work Report {YYYY-MM-DD}.md` - **Overwrite** if the file already exists - Include all sections, tables, and suggestions exactly as generated - Reference: `workdir/❗ Daily Work Report.example.md` is a complete example of the expected output format and quality ## Error Handling - If any skill script fails, report the error clearly and continue with remaining sources - If env vars are missing (JIRA_EMAIL, JIRA_API_KEY, API_KEY_OTHER), report and skip that source - If Chrome CDP connection fails (gchat/gmail), report and skip those sources - Count items accurately for Quick Stats (count rows in tables, count work stream blocks, count bullet points) --- ## Save Report After generating the report, save it as: - **Path:** `workdir/❗ Daily Work Report {YYYY-MM-DD}.md` - **Overwrite** if the file already exists - Include all sections, tables, and suggestions exactly as generated - Reference: `workdir/❗ Daily Work Report.example.md` is a complete example of the expected output format and quality ## Error Handling - If any skill script fails, report the error clearly and continue with remaining sources - If env vars are missing (JIRA_EMAIL, JIRA_API_KEY, API_KEY_OTHER), report and skip that source - If Chrome CDP connection fails (gchat/gmail), report and skip those sources - Never silently skip data - always report what succeeded and what failed
